title: King & Pawn Endgames
subtitle: Opposition, triangulation, key squares, the square of the pawn, the outside passer, and the breakthrough — the bedrock of every endgame.

concepts.name: The Opposition
concepts.short: Kings facing on the same file/rank with one square between them — the side NOT to move holds the opposition and forces the other king to give way.

concepts.name: Key Squares
concepts.short: The squares in front of a pawn that win the game: if your king reaches one, the pawn promotes no matter whose move it is.

concepts.name: The Square of the Pawn
concepts.short: Draw the box from the pawn to its queening square; if the lone enemy king is inside it (or moves in on its turn) it catches the pawn — outside, the pawn runs and queens.

concepts.name: Triangulation
concepts.short: Lose a single tempo on purpose — walk your king around a triangle and return to the same square — so that the SAME position lands with the enemy to move. It hands him the zugzwang.

concepts.name: The Outside Passed Pawn
concepts.short: A passed pawn far from the other pawns: it decoys the enemy king to the rim, and your king feasts on the pawns it left behind.

concepts.name: The Breakthrough
concepts.short: A pawn sacrifice in a pawn race that smashes a path through the enemy pawns so one of yours queens first.

concepts.name: Converting K+P
concepts.short: King first, pawn second: shoulder the enemy king aside, win the key squares, and only then push the pawn — never the other way around.

lessons.title: The Opposition
lessons.summary: The single most important idea in king-and-pawn play: whoever does NOT have to move wins the standoff.

intro.text: Welcome to the engine room of chess. Every game, simplified far enough, becomes a king and a pawn. Master this and you stop drawing won games. Three to warm the hands.
Two kings, one pawn. The whole result turns on one word: opposition. Let me show you exactly what it means.

coach.text: Kings face each other on the e-file with one square between them. That is the opposition. The cruel truth: the side that must MOVE here is worse — the moving king has to step aside and let the enemy through.

It is White to move — so Black holds the opposition. White's king cannot advance: e6 is covered by the black king. Whatever White tries, Kd5 or Kf5, Black answers by facing him again. This is a DRAW, with a whole extra pawn.

Now the SAME position but Black to move — White holds the opposition. Black must abandon the file: ...Kd7 or ...Kf7. Then White outflanks to the other side — Kf6 or Kd6 — wins a key square, and the pawn promotes. Same board, opposite result. The move matters more than the material.

intro.text: Your turn. White to move, Black king floating back on e8. Don't lunge with the pawn — step the KING forward and grab a winning square.

coach.text: Black's king is one rank too far back, so it is NOT opposing you. Advance your king to take a key square in front of the pawn. Which step wins?

successLine.text: Yes. The king reaches the sixth rank ahead of its pawn — and once the king stands on a key square, the pawn queens by force. King first, pawn second. Always.

retryLine.text: Not that. Kd4, Kd5, Kf4, Kf5 all drift sideways or backward — Black answers ...Ke7 and takes the opposition, and you have thrown the win. March straight up the board: Ke6, Kd6, or Kf6.

intro.text: Each of these turns on the opposition or a single precise king step. Ask first: who is forced to give way?

lessons.title: Triangulation
lessons.summary: When the position is a mutual zugzwang and it is YOUR move, lose a tempo on purpose — walk the king around a triangle — and hand the bad move to your opponent.

intro.text: The opposition told you that having the move can be a curse. Triangulation is how you force that curse onto the enemy when it would otherwise fall on you.

coach.text: White king on d5, pawn on d4, kings facing on the d-file. This exact picture is a MUTUAL zugzwang: whoever is to move is worse. It is Black to move here — so Black is lost. He must step off the file, and then White's king outflanks to a key square and the pawn queens.

Now the SAME position with WHITE to move — and now WHITE is the one in trouble. Step to c5 or e5 and Black answers ...Kc7 / ...Ke7, holding the opposition; the pawn is stuck. White cannot make progress directly. The win is to give this very position back to Black — to lose exactly one tempo.

intro.text: Let me start from the position with BLACK to move — the losing side — and show how the zugzwang collapses. (To engineer this when it is YOUR move, you walk the king out and back along a three-square path so it returns to d5 having spent one extra tempo — and the identical position lands on Black instead. Here that work is done; watch the break.)

coach.text: Black is in zugzwang and must give way. ...Kc7 keeps the king as close as it can — but it has abandoned the d-file.
White does NOT rush the pawn. He sidesteps to outflank — the king is still the leader.
Black trails along, but he can never recover the opposition now — White has the move-advantage that triangulation manufactured.
Only NOW the pawn advances, shielded by its king. From here d5-d6-d7-d8 runs home; the black king is a step too slow to ever block it.

lessons.title: Key Squares
lessons.summary: The squares that win the game outright — reach one with your king and the pawn promotes regardless of whose move it is.

intro.text: The opposition is the tool. Key squares are the target. Let me name them.

coach.text: For a pawn that has not yet crossed the middle, the three key squares are the squares two ranks ahead of it — here d6, e6, f6. My king already stands on one of them, e6. From a key square the win is automatic: it no longer matters whose move it is.

Proof: White to move plays e5, then escorts the pawn home with the king shouldering Black away. But it would win with BLACK to move too — that is what 'key square' means. The king does the work; the pawn just walks behind it.

segments.title: Outflank to the free key square

intro.text: White to move. This time the black king is awake — it stands on d7, guarding two of your three key squares. Only ONE key square is still open to you. Find it.

coach.text: Your key squares are d6, e6, f6. But the black king on d7 covers d6 and e6 — step onto either and you would be standing next to his king, which is illegal anyway. So outflank AWAY from him. Which key square is free?

successLine.text: Kf6! You sidestepped the defender and planted your king on the one key square he could not stop. From f6 the e-pawn marches home and promotes by force — that is the whole power of a key square. Always outflank toward the side the enemy king cannot cover.

retryLine.text: Not there. The black king on d7 guards d6 and e6, so those are off limits. Pushing the pawn or drifting back lets ...Kd7-e7 hold the opposition and draw. Outflank to the free key square: Kf6.

segments.title: Cool-down: king to the key square

intro.text: Find the precise king move that wins the pawn race or holds the draw. Count the key squares before you move.

lessons.title: The Square of the Pawn
lessons.summary: A lone king versus a runner: draw the box and you instantly know whether the king catches the pawn or whether it queens.

intro.text: No kings to help the pawn, no kings to help the runner — just one king chasing one pawn. There is a shortcut so you never have to count squares move by move.

coach.text: Pawn on a4, queening on a8. Draw a square whose side runs from the pawn to its promotion square — a4 to a8 is four ranks, so the box is a4-a8-e8-e4. If the enemy king is inside that box (or can step in on its move), it catches the pawn. If not, the pawn runs and queens.

Black to move, king on e1 — just outside the box. It cannot get in: ...Kd2 leaves it on the e-to-d border one tempo short, and White simply pushes a5, a6, a7, a8=Q. Outside the square, the king is a spectator.

Compare: here the king starts on d5 — INSIDE the box. Now ...Kc6, ...Kb7, and the pawn is rounded up. Same pawn, the king one file closer, and the result flips from win to draw. The box tells you instantly which it is.

intro.text: White to move. The black king sits right on the edge of the square. One tempo decides everything — and a king move would waste it.

coach.text: Black's king is exactly on the border of the pawn's box. If you dawdle with a king move, ...Kc6 steps inside and the pawn falls — a draw. So don't dawdle. What wins?

successLine.text: Push! f5-f6 shrinks the box by one and the king can no longer reach it. The pawn queens by a single tempo. In a race with a lone king, you push the pawn, you do not shepherd it.

retryLine.text: Any king move loses the only thing that matters here — a tempo. Black replies ...Kc6, climbs into the square, and draws. The pawn must run NOW: f6.

intro.text: Promotion races and lone-king chases. Picture the box before you commit.

lessons.title: The Outside Passed Pawn
lessons.summary: A passer far from the action is worth more than an extra pawn in the centre — it drags the enemy king away so yours can feast.

intro.text: Equal pawns can still be a winning endgame. The trick is WHERE your extra resource sits.

coach.text: The kingside is balanced — White g4/h3 versus Black g6/h6. But White has a free a-pawn on the far wing with no black pawn near it. That is the outside passed pawn. It is far from both kings, and that distance is the whole point.

intro.text: Let me run the winning mechanism. Notice: the a-pawn never even needs to queen. Its job is to pull the black king to the rim.

coach.text: a5! The pawn marches. Black's king MUST chase it — if it ignores the runner, the pawn simply queens.
Black gives chase. Every step the black king takes toward a5 is a step away from its own kingside pawns.
Keep pushing. The decoy must look unstoppable so the king cannot turn back.
The black king is now stranded on the queenside, two files from home. Watch what White's king does.

White's king turns the OTHER way and walks into the kingside. The black king is stranded far on the queenside, so White's king and his g- and h-pawns now rule that wing unopposed — one of them queens long before Black can race back. The outside passer wins by being a decoy the enemy cannot ignore: it never even has to promote itself.

intro.text: Find the move that creates or runs the decisive passed pawn. Ask which pawn drags the enemy king furthest.

lessons.title: The Breakthrough
lessons.summary: When kings are too far to help, a pawn sacrifice can rip open a path and queen one pawn out of a frozen mass.

intro.text: Sometimes a wall of pawns looks blocked — but it is hiding a forced queen. The kings are out of the picture here; this is pure pawn arithmetic.

coach.text: Three white pawns face three black ones, kings marooned in the corners. It looks dead level. But White to move has a forced breakthrough — a sacrifice that clears a lane to a8.

The key is the MIDDLE pawn: b6!! After ...axb6 the a-file is open, so a5-a6 and the pawn runs in. After ...cxb6, the c-file opens for c5-c6. Either capture, White queens; declining loses to bxc7 or bxa7. The centre pawn is sacrificed to split the defenders.

intro.text: Same engine, your hands now. The kings can't reach the queenside in time. Smash through.

coach.text: Three on three, level material. Only ONE move wins — a sacrifice in the centre that opens a file no matter how Black recaptures. Find it.

successLine.text: b6!! The only winning move. Both recaptures open a lane for a flank pawn to queen, and declining drops a pawn with a winning pass anyway. Pushing a6 or c6 instead lets Black hold — the breakthrough must come through the middle.

retryLine.text: Pushing a flank pawn (a6 or c6) lets Black capture and seal the position — only a draw. The sacrifice must be the CENTRE pawn, b6, so that whichever way Black takes, a file opens for a runner.

intro.text: Pawn endings with a hidden breakthrough or a decisive pass. Look for the sacrifice that clears a path.

lessons.title: Practice & the Conversion
lessons.summary: Tie it together, then convert a won king-and-pawn ending against resistance.

intro.text: Opposition, key squares, the square, the passer, the breakthrough — they all live in these. One last round before the test of skill.

bossIntro.text: You are White. The f-pawns are locked, but you have the outside a-pawn — a winning advantage if you handle it correctly. Push it to drag the black king to the queenside, then turn your own king around and win the f5-pawn. King first when you can, pawn first in the race. Show me you have learned it.

questions.prompt: Two kings face each other on the same file with exactly one square between them. Who is better off?
options: The side to move, because it can choose the plan
The side NOT to move — the other king must step aside
Whoever has more space behind the king
It is always equal regardless of the move
questions.explain: That is the opposition: the player forced to move must give way, letting the opposing king advance. Having the move is a liability here.

questions.prompt: For a White pawn on e4 that has not yet crossed the middle, which squares are its key squares — the ones that win if your king reaches them whoever is to move?
options: e7 and e8 only
any square on the e-file
questions.explain: The key squares sit two ranks ahead of such a pawn: d6, e6, f6. A king on any of them escorts the pawn home regardless of the move.

questions.prompt: What is the practical point of the 'square of the pawn'?
options: It marks where the enemy king should hide
It tells you at a glance whether a lone king can catch a running pawn
It shows where to place your own king to defend
It is the set of squares the pawn must never enter
questions.explain: Draw the box from the pawn to its queening square: if the lone enemy king is inside it (or can step in on its move), it catches the pawn; outside, the pawn queens.

questions.prompt: Why is an OUTSIDE passed pawn often worth more than an extra central pawn in a king-and-pawn ending?
options: It promotes faster because it is closer to the edge
It decoys the enemy king to the rim, leaving the rest of the board to your king
It can never be captured on the a- or h-file
It gives check on promotion
questions.explain: The far passer drags the enemy king away to stop it; your king then walks the other way and harvests the pawns the enemy abandoned. The pawn often need not even queen.

questions.prompt: You reach a mutual-zugzwang king-and-pawn position, but it is YOUR move — so you would be the one forced to give way. What does triangulation achieve?
options: It promotes the pawn two squares at once
You lose a single tempo with your king so the same position returns with your OPPONENT to move
It forces a trade of the last pawns
It puts your king on the eighth rank
questions.explain: Triangulation means walking your king around a triangle to return to the same square, having spent one extra move. The identical position then lands with the opponent to move — and now HE is in the zugzwang.

questions.prompt: White to move. The pawn is on f4, the black king hangs back on f8 — too far to oppose you. Make the move that grabs a winning key square.
questions.explain: The key squares for the f4-pawn are e6, f6, g6. Ke6 or Kf6 lands on one; from there the pawn promotes by force. Pushing f4-f5 first would let ...Kf7/...Ke7 take the opposition and draw.

questions.prompt: White to move. The pawn is on c5; the lone black king on g6 sits one file outside the pawn's square. Find the only move that wins the race.
questions.explain: c6! shrinks the square so the king can never get in — the pawn queens by a single tempo. Any king move wastes that tempo and lets ...Kf6 step into the box and catch the pawn for a draw.

questions.prompt: White to move. Three pawns against three on the KINGSIDE, kings stuck in the corners, level material. Play the breakthrough that forces a queen.
questions.explain: g6!! — the CENTRE pawn — is the only winner. ...fxg6 opens the f-file for f6; ...hxg6 opens the h-file for h6; declining drops to gxf7/gxh7. Whichever way Black takes, a file opens and a flank pawn queens. Pushing f6 or h6 instead lets Black recapture and hold.

questions.prompt: White to move. You have the outside passed a-pawn while the kingside is balanced. Make the move that starts the decoy.
questions.explain: a5! runs the outside passer. Black's king must chase it, abandoning the kingside, after which White's king turns around and devours g6 and h6.

questions.prompt: Black was in a drawn pawn ending and just played ...Ke7. Correct defence, or a blunder?
questions.explain: ...Ke7 takes the frontal opposition and holds the draw. The defending king must step straight back to face the enemy king — that is the only saving move.

questions.prompt: From the same drawn ending Black instead played ...Kd7, stepping to the side. Correct, or a blunder?
questions.explain: ...Kd7? abandons the file. White answers Kf6!, outflanks to a key square, and wins. The defender must retreat STRAIGHT back (...Ke7), never sideways.

questions.prompt: Black's king was hanging back on e8. White played Ke5-f6, heading for a key square. Sound, or a blunder?
questions.explain: Kf6 reaches a key square in front of the e-pawn. From there White shoulders the black king away and the pawn promotes — the textbook king-first conversion.

questions.prompt: Black's king was inside the square of White's f-pawn and could catch it for a draw. Black just played ...Kb5, stepping AWAY from the pawn. Correct, or a blunder?
questions.explain: ...Kb5? steps out of the pawn's square. White plays f5, f6, f7, f8=Q and the king can never catch up. To hold, Black had to head TOWARD the pawn — ...Kd5 or ...Kd6 — staying inside the box.

questions.prompt: White to move. The black king is too far back to oppose you. Take the opposition-winning key square and seal the win.
questions.explain: Kd6 or Ke6 reaches a key square in front of the e-pawn; the king then shoulders the black king away and the pawn promotes. Drifting back or sideways would let ...Kd7/...Ke7 take the opposition.
